phase_outputs.iterate_scope:
Right — I don't have write access in this phase either, so this has to land as a clear spec for whoever implements it next.

Codex's read of the failure is the more credible one: this isn't a few stray errors, it's a missing layer. `NetworkEngine` and the view layer already assume a `LongwaveSimulation` package with a real domain model and protocol contract, and that package's actual type/engine files don't exist yet (or exist only partially) — so no amount of tweaking `NetworkEngine.swift` in isolation gets this to compile. The fix has to restore the boundary the rest of the code was already written against, not invent a new one. That said, my job as skeptic is to make sure "restore the missing layer" doesn't quietly become "redesign the missing layer" — so the instruction to the implementer is: match the shape `NetworkEngine`/`NetworkActioning` already expect (method names, error cases, snapshot/derived-state shape), don't invent a cleaner API in the process.

Two guardrails on top of Codex's list: (1) `DefaultSimulationEngine` and `LocalRuntimeServices` must be pure/deterministic — no direct `Date()`/`UUID()` calls outside `ClockProviding`, since offline-progress and idle-accrual math depend on that determinism and any drift there silently changes the game's economy, which is out of scope for a compile fix. (2) if implementing `resolveOffline`/`resolveRegionAccess` faithfully turns out to require guessing at balance numbers (accrual rates, region unlock thresholds) that aren't recoverable from existing call sites, that's the one case where "unavoidable" actually applies — flag it explicitly as a named placeholder rather than inventing plausible-looking numbers and letting them pass as decided design.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

Fix the compile break by restoring the missing `LongwaveSimulation` domain/engine layer that the existing UI and `NetworkEngine` already assume — this is a "fill in the missing boundary," not a rewrite. Concretely:

1. **`LongwaveSimulation/Package.swift`** — remove the deprecated `swiftLanguageMode` settings on the target and test target (hard blocker in the build log).
2. **`LongwaveSimulation/Sources/LongwaveSimulation/SimulationDomain.swift` (new)** — add the full set of domain types the rest of the codebase already references: `Coordinate`, `RegionID`, `RegionBounds`, `RelayType`, `Relay`, `DemandZone`, `RegionTopology`, `HealthLevel`, `RelayMetric`, `NetworkDerivedState`, `PlacementError` (incl. `.engineNotReady`), `PlacementOutcome`, `ElapsedResolution`, `OfflinePolicy`, `OfflineSummary`, `GameSnapshot`, `MilestoneState`, `ProHistoryBuffer`, `EntitlementState`, `RegionAccessState`, `LoadResult`, `RecoveryNotice`, `RecoveryMessage`, plus protocols `SnapshotStoring`, `EntitlementProviding`, `ClockProviding`, `SimulationEngine`, and the `resolveRegionAccess(...)` helper (fix the typo from `reolveRegionAccess`).
3. **`LongwaveSimulation/Sources/LongwaveSimulation/DefaultSimulationEngine.swift` (new)** — deterministic concrete `SimulationEngine`: `derive`, preview/commit for place/move/retune/upgrade, and `resolveOffline(...)`. No direct `Date()`/`UUID()` calls — route all time/identity through the injected `ClockProviding`/existing ID types so offline-accrual math stays reproducible.
4. **`Longwave/Engine/NetworkActioning.swift`** and **`Longwave/Engine/NetworkEngine.swift`** — add `import LongwaveSimulation`; otherwise leave logic untouched. `NetworkEngine`'s existing method names/signatures are the contract the domain layer must match, not the other way around.
5. **`Longwave/Engine/LocalRuntimeServices.swift` (new)** — minimal local implementations of `SnapshotStoring` (in-memory/on-disk store), `EntitlementProviding` (local-state entitlement, no real StoreKit calls needed for this pass), and `ClockProviding` (system clock wrapper).
6. **`Longwave/App/LongwaveApp.swift` (new)** — `@main` entry point wiring the engine + services into the environment, a bootstrap `.task`, and the existing root view (don't invent new screens here).

Order of work: get `Package.swift` fixing first, then the domain types, then the concrete engine/services, then the app entry point — each step should shrink the error count rather than shifting it around. Every mechanic already implied by `NetworkEngine`/the views (relay placement, signal propagation, upgrades, offline progress, region access/entitlement gating) must remain reachable from the UI after this pass — nothing gets stubbed out to make the compiler happy. If any offline/economy constant genuinely can't be inferred from existing call sites, implement it as an explicit placeholder value and call it out by name in the build report rather than silently deciding the game's balance.
phase_outputs.build_coordination:
Clean and consistent. This closes out the audit. Here's my integration summary.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

I went through this as the integrator, and since this session can't actually invoke the Swift compiler (build commands are blocked pending approval that never resolves), I did the next best thing: read every single `.swift` file in both the `LongwaveSimulation` package and the `Longwave` app target end-to-end, cross-checked every call site against the actual declared signatures, and ran a second independent audit agent over the same code to catch anything I missed. Between the two passes I found and fixed real bugs — this wasn't just a rubber-stamp of the prior workers' output.

**What I fixed as integrator:**
- `LongwaveSimulation/Package.swift` — removed the deprecated `swiftLanguageMode(.v5)` settings (the originally-named hard blocker), and added a placeholder test file so the test target isn't empty.
- Added the missing `import LongwaveSimulation` to `NetworkActioning.swift`, `NetworkEngine.swift`, and `PresentedSurface.swift` — these referenced package types but never imported the package.
- **`EntitlementProviding.restore()` signature mismatch**: `NetworkEngine.restorePurchases()` expected `restore()` to return an `EntitlementState`, but the protocol declared it as `Void`-returning. Fixed the protocol and `LocalEntitlementService`'s implementation to match what `NetworkEngine` (the frozen contract) actually calls.
- **`RecoveryNotice` pattern-match bug**: `NetworkEngine`'s `plainLanguage(for:)` matched `case .rolledBackToLastKnownGood:` on an enum case that has an associated value — invalid Swift. Fixed to bind and use the associated filename in the message.
- **Deleted `OfflineResolution.swift`/`OfflineResolutionSummary.swift`** — an orphaned, unreferenced duplicate of `DefaultSimulationEngine.resolveOffline` that referenced a nonexistent property (`NetworkHealthSnapshot` doesn't have `steadyStateCreditsPerSecond`). Nothing called it; the real, correct implementation is `DefaultSimulationEngine.resolveOffline`.
- **Three `Equatable`/`Codable` synthesis breaks**, all the same shape (a type declares conformance but a nested property doesn't): `RegionAccessState` wasn't `Equatable` even though `ProgressionScreen.swift` compares it with `==`; `MapOverlay: Equatable` and `RecoveryMessage: Equatable` couldn't synthesize because `OfflineSummary`/`RecoveryNotice` weren't `Equatable`; and critically, `GameSnapshot: Codable` (the type actually JSON-encoded/decoded to disk in `LocalSnapshotStore`) couldn't synthesize `Codable` because `OfflineSummary`/`ElapsedResolution` weren't `Codable`. Fixed the whole chain.
- Created a real `Assets.xcassets` (it existed as an empty folder with no `Contents.json` at all) with `AppIcon.appiconset` and `AccentColor.colorset`, since the build settings reference both by name.
- Verified the `.xcodeproj` itself is fine — it uses Xcode 16's synchronized-folder groups, so no manual pbxproj file-reference wiring was needed; I confirmed the scheme and target IDs are internally consistent.

**Where the build stands:** every mechanic named in the original scope — relay placement/move/retune/upgrade, signal/interference simulation, offline-progress accrual, milestones, region access/entitlement gating, save/recovery — is implemented, wired from `LongwaveApp` → `NetworkEngine` → `DefaultSimulationEngine`/`LocalRuntimeServices`, and reachable from the UI (`MapScreen`, `BuildInspectSheet`, `ProgressionScreen`, `PaywallScreen`, `SettingsScreen`). I read all of it and it's internally consistent.

**Honest caveat:** I could not get `swift build`/`xcodebuild` to actually run in this session (every attempt returned "command requires approval" with no way to grant it), so this is verified by careful manual + independent-agent static reading, not by an actual compiler pass. Someone with a real Xcode/swift toolchain should do one confirming `xcodebuild -scheme Longwave build` before calling this fully done — I'm confident but not 100% certain, since that's the nature of manual review vs. a compiler.

**Known, intentional gaps (not compile blockers):** the "Subscribe"/"Resubscribe" buttons on `PaywallScreen` are disabled with "coming soon" text — there's no real StoreKit purchase flow, only `restore()`, exactly as flagged by the prior worker as out of scope for this pass. `LongwaveTests`/`LongwaveUITests` folders are empty (pre-existing, not something this pass was asked to fill in). The app icon slot exists but has no actual artwork.

**Build verification:** still not compiling after 2 repair attempt(s)
phase_outputs.final_review:
Both participants agree on the substance — this is a no-go. The build still fails after two repair attempts, and every fix in this pass (including the integrator's very thorough manual reconstruction of the missing `LongwaveSimulation` layer) was made by reading code, not by running a compiler. That's a real weakness: two "this should fix it" attempts already turned out wrong, which means static reading alone isn't converging here. I'm going with Claude's framing over Codex's softer one — the honest state isn't "good reconstruction, needs one confirming build," it's "reconstruction is plausible but verification has failed twice and nobody has seen a real compiler error list yet." That's the version that won't mislead whoever picks this up next.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Go/no-go: NO-GO.** The app does not compile. It cannot be run or evaluated as a product in its current state.

**What was built:** The full missing `LongwaveSimulation` domain/engine layer was reconstructed from scratch to match the frozen `NetworkEngine`/`NetworkActioning` contract — all domain types (`Coordinate`, `Relay`, `RegionTopology`, `NetworkDerivedState`, `GameSnapshot`, etc.), the `SimulationEngine` protocol and a deterministic `DefaultSimulationEngine` (place/move/retune/upgrade, offline-progress resolution), local runtime services (`LocalSnapshotStore`, `LocalEntitlementService`, `SystemClock`), and the `@main` app entry point wiring it all together. The integrator's manual audit pass also fixed several real bugs found by close reading: an `EntitlementProviding.restore()` return-type mismatch, an invalid enum pattern match in recovery-notice handling, a chain of missing `Codable`/`Equatable` conformances that blocked snapshot persistence, and duplicate/orphaned files. On paper, every mechanic in scope — relay placement, signal/interference simulation, offline accrual, milestones, region-access/entitlement gating, save/recovery — is implemented and wired end-to-end into the UI.

**Verification status:** FAILED. `xcodebuild` for the iOS Simulator failed on the authoritative verification run, after two prior repair attempts (a `clampToRange` static-method scoping fix, a `Decimal`→`NSNumber` cast fix) each also failed verification. Nobody in this pipeline has been able to actually invoke `swift build`/`xcodebuild` and iterate against real compiler output — every attempt hit a permission wall, so all fixes so far have been educated guesses based on static code reading, not responses to an actual error list. That's the root cause of why this is stuck, not a shortage of engineering effort.

**Prompt coverage:** The request was narrow — "fix every compiler error until the build succeeds cleanly; do not drop features unless unavoidable." No features were dropped; the reconstructed domain layer is faithful to what the UI and `NetworkEngine` already expected. But the one measurable success condition — it builds — is not met, so prompt coverage on the thing that was actually asked for is 0%.

**Known limitations:** No real StoreKit purchase flow (Subscribe/Resubscribe buttons are intentionally disabled, restore-only) — pre-existing, out of scope for a compile fix. `LongwaveTests`/`LongwaveUITests` are empty. No app icon artwork. None of these matter yet, because the app cannot currently be opened at all.

**Top follow-ups, in order:**
1. **Blocker, needs an owner with real shell/toolchain access:** run `xcodebuild -scheme Longwave build` (or open in Xcode) and fix strictly off the literal first 10–20 diagnostics, in order — not off inference. This is the one thing that has been missing from every attempt so far.
2. Once compiling, do a quick smoke pass on app bootstrap, relay placement, offline-accrual math, and the save/recovery banner, since those were verified only by reading, never by running.
3. Only after a clean compile: decide whether MVP launch needs real StoreKit wiring or can ship restore-only, and fill in the empty test targets.

VERIFICATION: FAILED

error: release gate: last verification failed (compile FAILED for the iOS Simulator)
